nativeRange.habitat: Found in shallow outer reefs exposed to wave surge, typically in spur-and-groove zones — alternating ridges and channels of rock — with bare rock, rubble, and coral at depths from the surface down to about 20 m.

diet.notes: Feeds on a varied diet including squid, krill, clams, small fish, and hard-shelled shrimp. Hard-shelled prey is beneficial because it helps wear down the fish's constantly growing teeth. Wild populations have also been documented eating algae, mollusks, crustaceans, worms, echinoderms, sponges, and foraminiferans (single-celled organisms with hard shells), making the species broadly omnivorous in nature despite a predominantly meaty diet in captivity.

imageCandidates.notes: Photograph by Brian Gratwicke, originally posted to Flickr 2012-02-26. High resolution (3228 × 2198 px). Unambiguously identified as Rhinecanthus rectangulus on the Commons page.

Photo by J.E. Randall, Hawaii. FishBase states copyright remains with the photographer/institution; license not explicitly stated. Randall photos are generally considered reliable species identifications.
Product hero image from LiveAquaria species page. Clean, watermark-free retailer product photo. Recommended only as fallback if no CC-licensed image is suitable.

summary: Hawaii's state fish, the Humu Rectangle Triggerfish (Rhinecanthus rectangulus) is a bold reef predator with striking black, white, and yellow markings. It is widely considered hardy and easy to feed, though it tends to be aggressive and is not considered reef-safe.
careNotes:
The Humu Rectangle Triggerfish is widely considered a hardy and entertaining species, but it is generally advised to be kept in a large tank — at least 180 gallons (about 681 L) — given its adult size and territorial nature. It tends to rearrange rocks and decor to its liking, and has been reported to produce a grunting vocalization, particularly when disturbed or handled.

This species is not reef-compatible. It will generally consume invertebrates and has been known to disturb corals, so reef tanks and mixed invertebrate setups are best avoided. It tends to do well when kept with other large, semi-aggressive to aggressive fish of similar size. Adult specimens have been reported to become increasingly assertive as they mature.

Like other triggerfish, the Humu Rectangle can lock its first dorsal spine upright as a defense mechanism, wedging itself into crevices where it is difficult to dislodge. A well-secured lid is generally recommended, as triggerfish are capable of moving or dislodging equipment.

In captivity, feed a varied diet of meaty foods such as mysis shrimp, krill, and clams, with occasional algae offerings. Including hard-shelled prey such as whole shrimp or clams is beneficial for wearing down the fish's continuously growing teeth.
breedingNotes: Breeding in captivity has not been documented. In the wild, the species reproduces by laying eggs (oviparous reproduction) and exhibits distinct pairing behavior during spawning.

additional.notes: Used for taxonomy, native range, temperature range (wild), and diet classification. Species ID 5840. Temperature range (24.8–29.3°C) differs significantly from LiveAquaria aquarium recommendation (22.2–25.6°C).

additional.name: BlueZoo Aquatics
additional.notes: Cross-check for size, temperament, and tank size. Lists minimum tank as 50 gallons vs LiveAquaria's 180 gallons — significant disagreement noted in careNotes. Lists temperament as 'aggressive' vs LiveAquaria's 'semi-aggressive'.

fish.breedingNotes: Breeding in captivity has not been documented. In the wild, spawning is oviparous (egg-laying) with distinct pairing behavior.
